metadata.download_pdf: /downloads/from-the-classroom/Antje van Essen-Hermans - Innovate for Impact - Amsterdam UAS.pdf

course_overview.title: Innovate for Impact
course_overview.typical_student_background: Students work in teams to provide strategic, evidence-based marketing and sales advice to companies, focusing on innovation and measurable value creation. The course bridges academic theory with real-world business challenges.
course_overview.prerequisites: Completion of all specialisation courses in the International Marketing & Sales major.

raw.ai_usage_summary: AI was integrated throughout the course as a support tool for ideation, research, analysis, and design. Students used tools such as ChatGPT, ResearchRabbit, Gemini, Perplexity, Canva, and Qualtrics to develop marketing concepts, conduct research, analyse data, and create prototypes. A distinctive element was the use of AI as a 'devil's advocate' to challenge ideas and encourage reflection. The main goal was to enhance creativity and efficiency while keeping students critical and responsible in their use of AI.

outcomes: Imagine creative marketing opportunities with appeal to a target audience.
Define a complex marketing problem in an international business setting.
Develop a SMART method to approach complex marketing problems within a given timeframe.
Integrate insights from academic and professional sources in the analysis of complex marketing problems.
Design sustainable solutions to complex marketing problems by using the intervention cycle.
Demonstrate an evidence-based line of reasoning using primary and secondary data.
Use AI in the design and assessment of marketing solutions.
learning_outcomes.ai_support_per_ilo: AI supported creativity by helping students generate and test ideas for sustainable category extensions. It helped structure problem statements, research questions, and planning. AI tools also supported literature review, survey design, data analysis, and visual prototyping. Throughout the course, students documented and reflected on AI use, which supported critical evaluation and responsible application.
learning_outcomes.ai_specific_outcomes_description: One explicit learning outcome requires students to use AI in the design and assessment of marketing solutions. Students are also expected to document their AI use and reflect critically on how AI contributed to, limited, or biased their work.

purpose_and_benefits.main_reason: The main reason was to support creativity, research, and design in a real-world marketing project. AI was used to help students work more efficiently and to develop their ability to use AI critically in professional practice.
purpose_and_benefits.benefits_for_students: AI helped students generate ideas faster, structure research tasks, and create prototypes more easily. It increased engagement and made parts of the course more accessible, especially for students who needed support with writing, design, or research logic. It also encouraged experimentation and discussion. Students learned that AI works best when combined with human judgment and reflection.
purpose_and_benefits.benefits_for_lecturers: AI helped lecturers give clearer examples, support student ideation, and make discussions more concrete. It improved efficiency because students could produce draft ideas, analyses, and visual outputs more quickly, which made feedback more focused. However, lecturers also needed extra time to guide students in prompting, evaluation, and responsible use.

implementation_details.how_used_in_class: AI was used across different phases of the course. In the early weeks, students used AI for brainstorming category extensions, team activities, and problem definition. Later, they used it for literature search, research planning, SWOT analysis, survey design, data analysis, poster creation, and final presentations. AI use took place in lectures, workshops, group work, and individual reflection tasks.
implementation_details.access: Students mainly used a combination of free and paid versions of tools. In each group there would typically be students with paid versions. Most tools were optional rather than compulsory.
implementation_details.guidance_provided: Students received presentations on available platforms, demonstrations of prompting, in-class coaching, and feedback on their outputs. Reflection tasks were used to help students think critically about bias, quality, and reliability. Support was practical and ongoing, especially when students were new to specific tools.
implementation_details.assessment_details: AI usage was part of the assessment through rubric-based criteria. Students were expected to use at least two AI tools, document their interactions, and reflect critically on how AI contributed to creativity, research, and design.

evaluation_and_risk_management.impact_evaluated_how: The impact was evaluated through student AI usage logs, instructor reflections, feedback during coaching sessions, and review of student outputs. The course also planned pre- and post-course AI literacy surveys conducted by the AI-HED team.
evaluation_and_risk_management.metrics_or_kpis_description: Suggested indicators included student performance, engagement, quality of AI reflections, total number of students reporting AI as helpful, and number of AI-generated ideas that were used or revised.

risk_management.description: The main risks identified were overreliance on AI, uneven AI literacy, bias in outputs, superficial reflection, and technical or access issues. These were managed through lecturer guidance, peer support, structured prompting exercises, reflection tasks, and clear expectations that AI should support rather than replace student thinking. Students were encouraged to validate outputs and compare them with theory, evidence, and their own judgment.

overall_impact.impact_on_teaching_learning: Overall, AI had a positive impact on both teaching and learning. It increased engagement, supported creativity, and made several tasks more efficient, especially in ideation, research, and design. It also helped students participate more actively and made some complex tasks feel more accessible. At the same time, the course showed that AI only adds value when students are guided to question outputs, reflect critically, and use tools selectively. Challenges for lecturers included managing different student skill levels, supporting tool use, and dealing with technical limitations.
overall_impact.impact_on_student_outcomes: AI supported student learning outcomes in a mainly positive way. It improved idea generation, research structuring, survey development, and visual communication. Students also became more aware of bias, prompting, and the need to evaluate AI outputs. The strongest gains were in creativity, efficiency, and engagement, while the weakest area remained critical reflection, which still needed continuous support.

main_challenges.management: Addressed through demonstrations, in-class coaching, and peer learning.
Students were encouraged to use AI as a co-creator rather than a ready-made answer machine; reflection logs reinforced critical evaluation.

main_challenges.challenge: Limited free access to some tools
main_challenges.management: Optional rather than compulsory tool use reduced barriers; peer support within groups helped where access was unequal.
Managed through in-class coaching and flexible tool selection.
Reflection tasks and rubric criteria kept focus on critical evaluation of AI-generated content.

main_challenges.challenge: Students feeling overloaded by the number of available tools
main_challenges.management: Guidance focused on connecting a small number of tools clearly to learning outcomes.

scalability_and_transferability.scaling_requirements: This experience can be scaled to other project-based courses where students need support in ideation, research, analysis, and communication. To scale it well, lecturers would need basic training in AI tools and prompting, clear assessment criteria, and practical guidelines for responsible use.
scalability_and_transferability.key_advice: Start with a small number of tools and connect them clearly to the learning outcomes. Give students practical guidance early, especially on prompting, bias, and reflection. Keep AI use purposeful and do not let the tool drive the course. Students benefit most when AI is introduced as support for thinking, not as a substitute for it.

pull_quotes.speaker: Antje van Essen-Hermans

pull_quotes.text: AI only adds value when students are guided to question outputs, reflect critically, and use the tools selectively.
pull_quotes.speaker: Antje van Essen-Hermans
